howItWorks.intro: Your benefit starts from your Average Indexed Monthly Earnings (AIME), then runs through the bend-point formula to get your Primary Insurance Amount (PIA) — the monthly check you would receive at Full Retirement Age. Claiming earlier or later adjusts that PIA up or down.

rules.name: PIA Formula
rules.explanation: The progressive benefit formula replaces a higher percentage of lower earnings.

rules.name: Delayed Credits
rules.formula: 8% increase per year past FRA (up to age 70)
rules.explanation: Each year you delay past full retirement age adds 8% to your benefit permanently.

howItWorks.outro: The bend-point dollar amounts shift each year with wage growth, and the figures here use recent bend points. For the official number tied to your actual earnings record, pull your statement from ssa.gov.

faq.question: What is Full Retirement Age (FRA)?
faq.answer: FRA is the age at which you collect 100% of your Primary Insurance Amount with no reduction and no bonus. Anyone born in 1960 or later has an FRA of 67. People born 1955-1959 fall between 66 and 2 months and 66 and 10 months. Claiming before FRA shrinks the check; claiming after grows it.

faq.question: How much do I lose by claiming at 62 instead of 67?
faq.answer: For someone with an FRA of 67, filing at 62 permanently cuts the benefit by about 30%. A $2,000 full-retirement check becomes roughly $1,400. The reduction is 5/9 of 1% per month for the first 36 months early and 5/12 of 1% per month beyond that.

faq.question: Is it worth waiting until 70?
faq.answer: Each year you delay past FRA adds 8% in delayed retirement credits, up to age 70. On a $2,000 FRA benefit, waiting from 67 to 70 raises the monthly check to about $2,480. Credits stop accruing at 70, so there is no reason to wait longer. Delaying pays off most if you expect a long life; claiming early pays more total if you do not live much past your late 70s.

faq.question: How is the benefit calculated from my earnings?
faq.answer: SSA indexes your highest 35 years of earnings for wage inflation, sums them, and divides by 420 months to get your AIME. The AIME runs through the bend-point formula — 90% of the first slice, 32% of the middle slice, 15% of the top slice — to produce your PIA. This tool approximates that using your average annual earnings rather than a year-by-year record.

faq.question: Is this the official amount I will receive?
faq.answer: No. This is a planning estimate built from average earnings and current bend points, not your certified benefit. The official figure comes from SSA using your actual indexed earnings record, cost-of-living adjustments, and any Medicare premium deductions. Check your personalized number at ssa.gov/myaccount before making a claiming decision.

faq.question: Can I keep working while collecting?
faq.answer: Yes, but if you claim before FRA and keep earning, SSA withholds $1 for every $2 above the annual earnings limit (about $23,400 in 2025). Once you reach FRA there is no penalty, and withheld amounts are credited back through a higher benefit.

howWeCalculate.intro: The tool converts your average earnings into an AIME, applies the bend-point formula for your PIA, then adjusts for the claiming age you pick relative to your Full Retirement Age.
steps: Your average annual earnings are divided by 12 to approximate your Average Indexed Monthly Earnings (AIME).
The AIME runs through the progressive bend-point formula — 90% of the lowest slice, 32% of the middle, 15% of the top — to produce your Primary Insurance Amount (PIA).
Your birth year sets your Full Retirement Age (67 for anyone born 1960 or later).
Claiming before FRA reduces the PIA (about 30% at 62); claiming after adds 8% per year in delayed credits up to age 70.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: This is an estimate for planning, not the official SSA figure. It uses average earnings instead of your full year-by-year record and does not reflect cost-of-living adjustments, taxation, or Medicare deductions. Confirm your actual benefit at ssa.gov.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this when you are weighing a claiming decision or sketching out retirement income and want to see how age and earnings move the monthly number.
scenarios: Deciding whether to file at 62, wait until your Full Retirement Age of 67, or hold out to 70 for the maximum check.
Estimating how a $2,000 full-retirement benefit shrinks to about $1,400 at 62 or grows to about $2,480 at 70.
Building a retirement budget and needing a monthly and annual benefit figure to plug in.
Comparing your own claiming age against a spouse's to coordinate household income and survivor benefits.
Seeing roughly where you land on the bend-point formula before pulling your official statement from ssa.gov.

sections.title: What This Calculator Estimates
sections.body: Social Security retirement benefits come from a specific chain: your top 35 years of earnings become your Average Indexed Monthly Earnings (AIME), the AIME runs through a bend-point formula to set your Primary Insurance Amount (PIA), and your claiming age raises or lowers that PIA. Enter your average annual earnings, your birth year, and the age you plan to file, and this tool walks that chain to show an estimated monthly and annual benefit. It is a planning estimate, not the certified figure SSA calculates from your actual earnings record.

sections.title: How Claiming Age Changes the Check
sections.body: The single biggest lever is when you file. For anyone born in 1960 or later, Full Retirement Age is 67. Claim at 62 and you take a permanent cut of roughly 30% — a $2,000 full-retirement benefit drops to about $1,400 a month. Wait past 67 and you earn delayed retirement credits of 8% per year up to age 70, so that same $2,000 benefit climbs to about $2,480. Filing early means smaller checks for more years; filing late means larger checks for fewer years, and where you come out ahead depends mostly on how long you live.

sections.title: The Bend-Point Formula in Plain Terms
sections.body: The PIA formula is deliberately progressive so it replaces more of a low earner's income than a high earner's. It credits 90% of the first slice of AIME, 32% of the middle slice, and only 15% of the top slice. That is why doubling your career earnings does not double your benefit — the extra dollars land in the 15% band. The dollar thresholds between those slices, called bend points, shift a little each year with national wage growth, so estimates will drift over time until you check your real number at ssa.gov.

howToSteps.title: Enter your average annual earnings
howToSteps.description: Use the average of your highest 35 earning years, in today's dollars. If some years were low or zero, they still count toward the 35, which pulls the average down.

howToSteps.title: Enter your birth year
howToSteps.description: This sets your Full Retirement Age. Anyone born in 1960 or later has an FRA of 67; earlier birth years fall between 66 and 2 months and 66 and 10 months.

howToSteps.title: Pick the age you plan to claim
howToSteps.description: Choose 62 for the earliest option, 67 for a full benefit, or 70 for the maximum. Each choice adjusts your Primary Insurance Amount up or down.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: The tool computes your AIME, applies the bend-point formula for your PIA, then adds the early reduction or delayed credit for your claiming age.

howToSteps.title: Compare a few claiming ages
howToSteps.description: Run 62, 67, and 70 back to back to see the spread between the smallest and largest check, then verify against your official statement at ssa.gov.

tips: Every year of near-zero earnings inside your top 35 drags the average down — the formula fills any missing years with zeros.
Delayed retirement credits stop at age 70, so there is no benefit to filing later than 70.
Between 62 and 67 the check grows roughly 6% to 8% for each year you wait; running each age shows exactly what a year of patience is worth.
If you are married, model both spouses' claiming ages together — the higher earner's decision also sets the survivor benefit.
Treat the result as a ballpark. Your official PIA depends on your full indexed earnings record and annual cost-of-living adjustments.
